description: Curated AuthClaw regulatory corpus for GDPR, HIPAA Security Rule, and SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria retrieval. Passages are concise operational summaries with official source citations.

sections.title: Article 5 - Processing Principles
sections.text: Personal data processing must be lawful, fair, transparent, purpose limited, data minimized, accurate, storage limited, integrity/confidentiality protected, and accountable. AuthClaw controls should prove purpose limitation, redaction, auditability, and retention discipline.

sections.title: Article 25 - Data Protection By Design And By Default
sections.text: Controllers must apply appropriate technical and organizational measures, including pseudonymization and default minimization, so only necessary personal data is processed. AuthClaw remediation should prefer privacy-by-default routes, minimized prompts, scoped access, and reversible token controls only where justified.

sections.title: Article 32 - Security Of Processing
sections.text: Security measures should reflect risk and may include pseudonymization, encryption, confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience, restoration capability, and regular testing. AuthClaw evidence should tie redaction, key rotation, audit integrity, recovery, and tests to risk reduction.

sections.title: Article 30 - Records Of Processing Activities
sections.text: Organizations must maintain processing records describing purposes, categories of data, recipients, transfers, retention periods, and security measures. AuthClaw evidence exports should preserve policy versions, gateway routes, approvals, redaction metrics, and audit-chain proof.

sections.title: 45 CFR 164.308 - Administrative Safeguards
sections.text: Administrative safeguards require security management processes, assigned responsibility, workforce controls, information access management, awareness training, incident procedures, contingency planning, evaluation, and business associate controls. AuthClaw should show owner assignment, approval workflows, audit trails, and repeatable review evidence.

sections.title: 45 CFR 164.312 - Technical Safeguards
sections.text: Technical safeguards cover access control, unique user identification, emergency access, automatic logoff, encryption/decryption, audit controls, integrity controls, authentication, and transmission security. AuthClaw evidence should connect API key lifecycle, MFA approvals, PHI redaction, audit hash-chain integrity, and provider credential protection.

sections.title: 45 CFR 164.310 - Physical Safeguards
sections.text: Physical safeguards address facility access controls, workstation use, workstation security, and device/media controls. In cloud-native AuthClaw deployments, evidence should map hosting controls, endpoint restrictions, backup handling, and infrastructure access to the covered environment.

sections.title: 45 CFR 164.316 - Policies, Procedures, And Documentation
sections.text: Covered entities must maintain required policies, procedures, and documentation, retain them for required periods, make them available to responsible persons, and review/update them as needed. AuthClaw should preserve policy-as-code versions, runbooks, approvals, and evidence packages.

sections.title: Common Criteria CC6 - Logical And Physical Access Controls
sections.text: Logical access controls should restrict system access to authorized users and protect credentials through provisioning, authentication, authorization, review, and removal. AuthClaw evidence should include API key expiry/rotation, scoped roles, provider credential rotation, tenant isolation, and access review artifacts.

sections.title: Common Criteria CC7 - System Operations
sections.text: System operations criteria emphasize detection, monitoring, incident response, and corrective action. AuthClaw evidence should connect gateway telemetry, redaction fallback metrics, DLQs, audit chain verification, alert runbooks, and remediation action audit events.

sections.title: Common Criteria CC8 - Change Management
sections.text: Change management criteria expect controlled development, testing, approval, and deployment of changes. AuthClaw should preserve policy validation, dry-run decisions, version activation/rollback, CI evidence, Terraform plans, and reviewed deployment history.

sections.title: Confidentiality Category
sections.text: Confidentiality commitments require identifying confidential information, protecting it through its lifecycle, limiting access and disclosure, and securely disposing of it. AuthClaw should map redaction, token retention/purge, encryption, provider controls, and audit exports to confidentiality commitments.
